questions.stem: Which groups are attached to the central (alpha) carbon of a generalized amino acid?

options.text: An amine group, a carboxyl group, an R-group and a hydrogen
Two amine groups and two carboxyl groups
A phosphate, a sugar and a base
Three fatty acids and a glycerol

questions.explanation: A generalized amino acid has an alpha carbon bonded to an amine group, a carboxyl group, an R-group and a hydrogen.

questions.stem: The part of an amino acid that varies between the different amino acids is the:
questions.explanation: The R-group (side chain) differs between amino acids, while the amine, carboxyl and alpha carbon are common to all.

questions.stem: What is formed, and by what reaction?
questions.explanation: Two amino acids join by a condensation reaction to form a dipeptide, releasing water.

questions.stem: The bond formed between two amino acids is called a:
questions.explanation: Amino acids are linked by peptide bonds formed in condensation reactions.

questions.stem: Essential amino acids are those that:

options.text: Cannot be synthesized and must come from the diet
Can be made from other amino acids
Are never needed by the body

questions.explanation: Essential amino acids cannot be made by the body and must be obtained from food.

questions.scenario: A person follows a vegan diet.
questions.stem: Why must they pay attention to their protein sources?

options.text: To ensure all essential amino acids are consumed
Because plants contain no amino acids

questions.explanation: Vegan diets require attention to ensure all essential amino acids are obtained from plant sources.

questions.stem: Non-essential amino acids are those that the body can:
questions.explanation: Non-essential amino acids can be synthesized in the body from other amino acids.

questions.stem: How many different amino acids are coded for in the genetic code?
questions.explanation: Twenty amino acids are coded for in the genetic code.

questions.stem: Why is there an almost infinite variety of possible polypeptides?

options.text: Chains can be any length with amino acids in any order

questions.explanation: With 20 amino acids in any order and chains of any length, the number of possible peptides is effectively limitless.

questions.scenario: An enzyme is heated to a high temperature and stops working.
questions.stem: What has most likely happened to the protein?

options.text: It has been hydrolysed into glucose

questions.explanation: High temperature can denature a protein, changing its shape so it no longer functions.

questions.stem: Denaturation of a protein involves:

options.text: A change in its three-dimensional shape
A change in its amino acid sequence

questions.explanation: Denaturation (by pH or temperature) alters the protein's shape without changing its sequence of amino acids.

questions.stem: Which two factors are highlighted as affecting protein structure?
questions.explanation: Both pH and temperature can affect protein structure, potentially causing denaturation.

questions.stem: During the formation of a dipeptide, which molecule is released?
questions.explanation: Peptide bond formation is a condensation reaction that releases a molecule of water.

questions.stem: The carboxyl group of an amino acid contains which atoms?
questions.explanation: The carboxyl group is -COOH; the amine group is -NH2.

questions.stem: A polypeptide chain can contain how many amino acids?
questions.explanation: Peptide chains can have any number of amino acids, from a few to thousands.

questions.scenario: Egg white turns solid and opaque when cooked.
questions.stem: This is an everyday example of:
questions.explanation: Cooking denatures the egg proteins, changing their shape and appearance.

questions.stem: Which statement about dietary amino acids is correct?

options.text: Essential ones must come from food; non-essential ones can be synthesized
All amino acids can be synthesized by humans
No amino acids are needed in the diet

questions.explanation: Essential amino acids must be eaten, while non-essential ones can be made from other amino acids.

questions.stem: The amine group of an amino acid is written as:
questions.explanation: The amine group is -NH2; the carboxyl group is -COOH.

questions.stem: A chain of many amino acids joined by peptide bonds is a:
questions.explanation: Many amino acids linked by peptide bonds form a polypeptide.

questions.stem: The huge diversity of proteins ultimately arises from variation in the:

options.text: Sequence and number of amino acids

questions.explanation: Proteins vary because amino acids can be in any order and chains can be any length.

questions.scenario: A protein is exposed to a very low pH.
questions.stem: What is the likely effect?
questions.explanation: Extreme pH, like extreme temperature, can denature a protein by disrupting its structure.

questions.stem: To break a polypeptide back into individual amino acids, the reaction needed is:
questions.explanation: Hydrolysis breaks peptide bonds using water, releasing individual amino acids.

questions.stem: Which of these is common to every amino acid?

options.text: An amine group and a carboxyl group

questions.explanation: All amino acids share an amine group and a carboxyl group on the alpha carbon, differing only in their R-group.

questions.stem: Which of these is an example of a polypeptide?
questions.explanation: Insulin is a polypeptide; glucose is a sugar, and the others are a lipid and a polysaccharide.

questions.stem: Which food-related issue relates most directly to essential amino acids?

options.text: Ensuring a balanced protein intake in the diet

questions.explanation: Because essential amino acids cannot be synthesized, a balanced protein intake is needed to supply them.

questions.stem: Which statement about denaturation is correct?

options.text: It changes shape and function, not the amino acid sequence
It always changes the amino acid sequence

questions.explanation: Denaturation disrupts the 3D shape and function while leaving the primary sequence intact.

questions.scenario: A diagram shows a central carbon bonded to four different groups.
questions.stem: For it to be a generalized amino acid, one of those groups must be a:
questions.explanation: The alpha carbon carries the amine group, carboxyl group, a hydrogen and a variable R-group.

questions.stem: The word equation 'amino acid + amino acid → dipeptide + water' describes a:
questions.explanation: The release of water as two amino acids join shows this is a condensation reaction.

questions.stem: If two polypeptides have the same amino acids but in a different order, they will:
questions.explanation: Order matters: the same amino acids in a different sequence produce a different protein.

questions.stem: Which best describes why essential amino acids are called 'essential'?

options.text: They are essential in the diet because the body cannot make them
They are the only amino acids that exist
They are not needed for proteins
They are made only from lipids

questions.explanation: They must be supplied in the diet because they cannot be synthesized in the body.

questions.stem: A tripeptide is formed from three amino acids. How many peptide bonds does it contain?
questions.explanation: Joining three amino acids requires two peptide bonds (n amino acids form n−1 bonds).

questions.stem: Enzymes are proteins, so extreme heat is likely to:

options.text: Denature them and stop them working

questions.explanation: Because enzymes are proteins, high temperatures can denature them, disrupting their active sites.

questions.stem: The number of possible peptide sequences is enormous mainly because:

options.text: Each position can be any of 20 amino acids
Only one amino acid is used
Chains are always the same length

questions.explanation: With 20 choices at each of many positions, the number of possible sequences becomes astronomically large.

questions.stem: The chemical diversity of proteins arises largely from the variety of amino acid:
questions.explanation: R-groups vary chemically and determine the properties of assembled polypeptides, giving proteins their diversity.

questions.stem: Hydrophilic R-groups are:
questions.explanation: Hydrophilic R-groups are polar or charged (acidic or basic), while hydrophobic R-groups are non-polar.

questions.stem: What ultimately determines the three-dimensional shape of a protein?

options.text: The temperature of the room only

questions.explanation: The sequence and precise position of each amino acid (primary structure) determines the protein's 3D conformation.

questions.stem: Because their shapes are determined by their sequence, proteins have structures that are:
questions.explanation: Despite their complexity, proteins fold into precise, predictable and repeatable structures determined by their sequence.

questions.stem: Secondary structure of a protein, such as an alpha helix or beta-pleated sheet, is stabilized by:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding in regular positions stabilizes secondary structures such as alpha helices and beta-pleated sheets.

questions.stem: Which of these are examples of protein secondary structure?
questions.explanation: Alpha helices and beta-pleated sheets are the two main types of secondary structure.

questions.scenario: Two cysteine amino acids form a strong covalent link in a protein.
questions.stem: This link is a:
questions.explanation: Pairs of cysteines form disulfide covalent bonds, one of the interactions stabilizing tertiary structure.

questions.stem: Which of these does NOT contribute to the tertiary structure of a protein?
questions.explanation: Tertiary structure depends on hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, disulfide covalent bonds and hydrophobic interactions, not glycosidic bonds.

questions.stem: Ionic bonds in tertiary structure form when R-groups:

options.text: Become positively or negatively charged and attract each other

questions.explanation: Amine and carboxyl groups in R-groups can gain or lose hydrogen ions to become charged and form ionic bonds.

questions.scenario: A globular protein is dissolved in water.
questions.stem: Where are its hydrophobic amino acids most likely located?

options.text: Clustered in the core, away from water
On the outer surface facing water

questions.explanation: In water-soluble globular proteins, hydrophobic amino acids cluster in the core away from water.

questions.scenario: An integral membrane protein sits within the phospholipid bilayer.
questions.stem: Its membrane-spanning region is likely to have:
questions.explanation: Integral proteins have regions of hydrophobic amino acids that help them embed in the membrane's hydrophobic core.

questions.scenario: Haemoglobin contains a non-protein haem group.
questions.stem: Haemoglobin is therefore an example of a:

options.text: Fibrous protein with no quaternary structure

questions.explanation: Haemoglobin is a conjugated protein because it includes a non-protein (haem) component; insulin and collagen are non-conjugated.

questions.stem: Quaternary structure refers to:

options.text: The association of two or more polypeptide subunits

questions.explanation: Quaternary structure is the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits (and any non-protein groups) in a protein.

questions.stem: Which pairing of protein type and example is correct?

options.text: Both collagen and insulin are fibrous

questions.explanation: Collagen is fibrous and structural; insulin is globular. Their shapes suit their functions.

questions.stem: Globular proteins differ from fibrous proteins in that they are generally:

options.text: Rounded and often soluble, with metabolic roles

questions.explanation: Globular proteins are compact and often soluble (e.g. insulin), whereas fibrous proteins are elongated and structural (e.g. collagen).

questions.scenario: Cryogenic electron microscopy has allowed imaging of single protein molecules.
questions.stem: This illustrates that technology can:

options.text: Reveal structures impossible to observe with the unaided senses

questions.explanation: Technologies such as cryo-EM allow imaging of structures, like single proteins, that cannot be seen with the unaided senses.

questions.scenario: A single amino acid in a protein's sequence is changed.
questions.stem: What is the possible consequence?

options.text: The protein's 3D shape and function may change
The number of subunits always doubles

questions.explanation: Because primary structure determines conformation, changing one amino acid can alter the protein's shape and function.
